summary: Justin Brennan walks through a real live deal that his mentee Michael is pursuing in Indianapolis—a 33-unit value-add apartment complex. The deal doesn't pencil at the asking price of $3.65M, but works at ~$3.1M with a 6.2% cap rate. Justin reviews underwriting fundamentals, value-add strategies (washer/dryer connections, amenity upgrades), and broker relationship dynamics while showing raw footage from on-site property tours.
takeaways: Washer-dryer connections cost $3,700-$5,000 per unit and generate ~$100/month rent bump—highest ROI value-add in older buildings
Frame laundry closets in kitchen nooks next to drainage/water lines and electrical panel; lose nook space but rent justifies it
Never pay asking price if numbers don't work; at $3.65M this deal yields only 1.1% cash-on-cash return, uninvestable
Only boost rents with major value-add (kitchens/baths) if you can increase rent >$200/month; otherwise it's not worth the capex spend
Kid play areas cost minimum $15-25K installed by certified vendors for liability compliance; dog parks are cheaper with basic 4-foot chain-link fencing
When touring: visit 1-2 units per type (1BR, 2BR, 3BR), takes 30-45 minutes total; don't tour all units until you're in escrow doing due diligence
Don't believe broker's verbal comp analysis—do your own homework on rental comps, location, amenities vs. nearby competition (Auburn Place had pool/gym here)
Current market cap rate floor is 6.0%+ Midwest/South; anything sub-6% is not investable given today's rate environment (10-year Treasury ~4.2-4.3%)
Smaller loans ($1-2M range) get 6.75% rates from local banks; larger loans ($3M+) qualify for agency debt (Fannie/Freddie) at 5.5-6.1%
Location aerial review: check for commercial/industrial buffer, train tracks, liquor stores, gas stations, crime zones, curb appeal, single-family residential nearby
Pre-market access requires existing deal history with broker; new investors can get week head-start via Facebook group + website vs. 2-3 week private list
Equity multiple = annual returns ÷ cash equity invested; at 30% down with 7030 split, investors should see ~14%+ annual return + 2x equity multiple

skill_candidates.change: Establish 6.0%+ cap rate floor as non-negotiable threshold for all markets before writing LOI
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin repeats 'you should not be buying anything sub-six anywhere in the Midwest...most cap rates are six to seven' and shows that at asking $3.65M, even with perfect assumptions the deal yields only 1.1% cash-on-cash, making it uninvestable

skill_candidates.change: Create rent-increase thresholds in underwriting template: only pursue capex value-adds if rent uplift exceeds $200/month minimum
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin states: 'you would do more value ad to it typically...only when you can boost the rents north of $200 a month that you would do more value ad'

skill_candidates.change: Require expense validation against per-unit benchmarks ($750-800/unit/year maintenance, $200-250/unit/year turnover) before accepting broker OM numbers
skill_candidates.evidence: When reviewing the 33-unit deal, Justin noted maintenance was running $900/unit/year (high) and flagged missing turnover budget, saying 'this number should be lower anyways'

skill_candidates.change: Run aerial/Google Street View assessment before LOI to identify location hazards: train tracks, industrial zoning, liquor stores, gas stations, setbacks from street
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin compared two Indianapolis properties via aerial—33-unit on quiet single-family street (good) vs 100-unit tucked behind industrial/gas station/train tracks (bad), saying 'it's a big no no...you can't even get to it without going through commercial and industrial'

skill_candidates.change: Accept lower-level associate brokers and don't expect 'head guy' access until after first deal; build team relationships systematically
skill_candidates.evidence: Michael found Tony (associate) didn't know property details, and Justin explained: 'you probably won't get to the top guy until you've done a deal...you'll get the lower level associate...that means he didn't know the property that well because it probably wasn't his listing'

title: Tuesday Mentorship Q&A - Apartment Deal Q&A_08-12-25 | Justin Brennan
